title_en: Unknown Narrators and the Problem of Anonymity
content_en:
A substantial portion of Mizan al-I'tidal addresses the category of unknown narrators (majhul or mubham) — those who appear in hadith chains but about whom the biographical literature provides little or no information. The handling of such narrators is one of the most technically complex aspects of hadith criticism, and al-Dhahabi's treatment is among the most systematic in the tradition.

Degrees of Anonymity

Al-Dhahabi distinguishes several degrees of anonymity among narrators. The first is the narrator known only by name (majhul al-ayn) — mentioned in one or more hadith chains but with no biographical information available about his character or reliability. The second is the narrator whose identity is known but whose reliability has never been assessed (majhul al-hal) — someone who appears in chains cited by major narrators but whom no major critic has either praised or criticized. The third is the completely anonymous narrator (mubham) — someone referred to only as 'a man' or 'a woman' without even a name provided.

The legal consequences of each degree differ. A narrator of unknown identity cannot be evaluated at all, and hadiths where he appears in a chain are weakened by his presence. A narrator of unknown reliability is in a somewhat better position if he was cited by reliable narrators without criticism — the fact that reputable scholars transmitted from him can be taken as an implicit endorsement. The completely anonymous narrator weakens a chain even more severely than the named but unknown one.

The Ijmali Hadith: Summary Transmissions

A related issue is the treatment of ijmali (summary) reports where a narrator says 'one of the Companions told me' or 'a reliable person narrated to me' without specifying the individual. Al-Dhahabi explains that if the narrator himself is fully reliable and the formulation implies he is citing someone he trusts, such reports may be acceptable for moral and spiritual purposes even if they cannot serve as legal proofs. The distinction between using hadith for legal proofs and using it for encouragement and warning is again relevant here.

The Companion Generation

An important special case discussed by al-Dhahabi involves Companions of the Prophet — the first generation of Muslims who knew the Prophet personally. By the consensus of Sunni hadith scholars, all Companions are considered just (ahl al-adalah) by virtue of their honor of being the Prophet's Companions and the divine commendation of them in the Quran. Their reliability as a class does not need to be individually established.

However, al-Dhahabi notes that some names appearing in hadith chains with the designation 'Companion' are actually uncertain — scholars disagreed about whether specific individuals actually met the Prophet or were merely of the following generation (Successors, tabi'un). He includes entries on these disputed Companions, presenting the evidence for and against their companionship and the consequences for the hadiths they transmitted.

Women Narrators in Mizan al-I'tidal

One of the distinctive features of Mizan al-I'tidal noted by later scholars is that al-Dhahabi includes relatively few women narrators in the book, explaining in his preface that no woman has ever been accused of fabricating hadith — the presumption of reliability was essentially universal for women narrators. While some women narrators have been criticized for weakness or poor memory, al-Dhahabi considered this rare enough that the inclusion of women in a book focused on the criticized and disputed was unnecessary. This observation, while reflecting a historical generalization rather than an absolute claim, was taken by subsequent scholars as evidence of the particular integrity of female narrators in the Islamic tradition.
